segments.title: CVA-01 leaves a carrier-policy gap
segments.summary: The opening history ties CVA-01's cancellation to British global-role assumptions and the Royal Navy's loss of future carrier and Type 82 task-group anchors.
segments.body: This section frames the counterfactual around political procurement choices rather than a sudden new fleet. Dr. Clarke argues that CVA-01 was cancelled because ministers were narrowing Britain's role toward Europe, even though carrier aviation had been intended to replace some permanent East of Suez basing. The same decision also damaged the Type 82 path, leaving the Royal Navy without the planned command escorts for carrier and surface action groups.

evidence.note: The transcript introduces CVA-01 cancellation as a political decision about Britain's role in the world.
The transcript explains CVA-01's power-projection role and the linked loss of Type 82 task-group command ships.

segments.title: Real Invincibles set the baseline
segments.summary: A technical baseline reviews the historical Invincible class as small through-deck cruisers with Sea Dart, sonar, Harriers, Sea Kings, and an ASW-heavy air group.
segments.body: Before changing the design, the video walks through the actual Invincible class: displacement, length, powerplant, sensors, sonar, ski jump, Sea Dart, and typical late-life air groups. The useful point for viewers is that the real ships were not large fleet carriers in miniature; they were compact, ASW-oriented aviation ships whose layout and air group constrained what could be changed plausibly. The Falklands comparison with Hermes also shows why aircraft capacity and helicopter mix matter more than a single tonnage label.

evidence.note: The transcript lists the historical Invincible class displacement, dimensions, machinery, sensors, sonar, ski jump, and Sea Dart fit.
The transcript compares Harrier, Sea King, and Hermes air-group capacity around the Falklands example.

segments.title: Australia and Canada need a carrier case
segments.summary: The Commonwealth path depends on Australia and Canada defending carrier aviation against budget, geography, and alliance arguments.
segments.body: This watch point explains why an eight-ship program cannot simply assign two carriers each to Australia and Canada. Australia has to justify naval aviation as defensive depth around a continent-sized maritime problem, while Canada has to explain why its smaller carrier contribution still matters beside the United States Navy. The segment is useful for understanding how alliance politics, sea blindness, and long-term naval investment shape whether a carrier program survives at all.

evidence.note: The transcript compares Australia and Canada losing carrier aviation and explains Australia's defensive-depth argument.
The transcript discusses Canada's alliance dilemma and the wider political difficulty of sustaining visible naval capabilities.

segments.title: Sea Harrier logic makes the hull grow
segments.summary: The larger Invincible route is anchored in Sea Harrier politics, V/STOL export appeal, and the flight-deck demands of a more ambitious Harrier path.
segments.body: The video uses the Sea Harrier program as the bridge between a cancelled conventional carrier and a larger through-deck cruiser. Dr. Clarke notes that a supersonic or more ambitious Harrier route would need more flight-deck length, giving the Navy a concrete reason to ask for a bigger Invincible. He also ties the aircraft to British industrial pride and export logic, which matters because procurement arguments often work better when they support jobs and visible national technology.

evidence.note: The transcript says a supersonic Harrier route would need a longer flight deck and could justify a larger Invincible class.
The transcript links Sea Harrier adoption to national pride, export appeal, and V/STOL airfield-survivability thinking.

segments.title: The 30,000-ton light-load Invincible
segments.summary: The alternate ship becomes a 30,000-ton light-load, 38,000-ton full-load carrier with Sea Dart, Sea Wolf, 24 Sea Harriers, 20 ASW Sea Kings, and four AEW Sea Kings.
segments.body: This is the core design payoff of the live stream. Dr. Clarke chooses 30,000 tons light load rather than full load, producing a much larger hull with more machinery, more electrical resilience, a longer flight deck, and a 48-aircraft group. The weapon and aircraft choices remain conservative: Sea Dart, Sea Wolf, Sea Harriers, and Sea Kings are all tied to systems already in British or Commonwealth procurement conversations rather than a clean-sheet fantasy fleet.

evidence.note: The transcript gives the alternate ship's displacement, dimensions, machinery, personnel, missile fit, air group, and flight deck.
The transcript explains ski-jump, Sea Wolf, Sea King AEW, ASW, and procurement-standardization choices.

segments.title: Sea King AEW wins the procurement argument
segments.summary: A procurement-focused section explains why a Sea King AEW path beats more exotic aircraft and how a larger Commonwealth fleet changes Harrier and helicopter production scale.
segments.body: The discussion is valuable because it keeps the alternate history constrained by training, logistics, and finance rather than choosing the most elegant aircraft on paper. Canada, Australia, and Britain already operate Sea King-family aircraft, so a Sea King AEW path is easier to sell than a new tilt-wing or fixed-wing support aircraft. The larger carrier force then changes aircraft economics, with hundreds of Sea Harriers, more Sea Kings, and possibly a different US Marine Corps or Sea Harrier II development path.

evidence.note: The transcript explains conservative procurement logic, Sea King commonality, and why a new aircraft would struggle politically.
The transcript estimates larger Sea Harrier and Sea King production and discusses allied and US Marine Corps effects.

segments.title: Falklands air defence changes sharply
segments.summary: The Falklands counterfactual argues that more Sea Harriers, AEW helicopters, and Sea Wolf self-defence could make the air campaign far harder for Argentina.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke expects the Falklands War still to happen because the diplomatic signalling problem remains, but the naval air picture changes. Instead of 28 Sea Harriers with no airborne early warning, the British could have 40 to 48 Sea Harriers plus AEW helicopters on Hermes and Invincible, making raids harder to surprise the task force. The section also explains how Sea Wolf on Invincible and more aircraft on combat air patrol would change the defence of Atlantic Conveyor, San Carlos, and the carrier group.

evidence.note: The transcript says the war likely still happens but with 40 to 48 Sea Harriers and airborne early warning changing air operations.
The transcript discusses Atlantic Conveyor, San Carlos, combat air patrols, Sea Wolf, and Hermes carrying a larger Harrier group.

segments.title: Escorts, exports, and replacement carriers
segments.summary: The closing analysis follows the larger carrier fleet into Ocean, Queen Elizabeth, Type 42/Type 23 escorts, Canadian and Australian escort options, and possible Indian or Brazilian export demand.
segments.body: The late stream widens the counterfactual from one ship design to a Commonwealth force-structure path. A larger Invincible fleet strengthens the case for retained Harriers, a larger amphibious aviation force, and perhaps a three-ship Queen Elizabeth replacement path rather than a two-carrier outcome. The Q&A tail then explores escort procurement: more Type 42s and Type 23s for Britain, possible Type 42 or Sea Dart-derived Canadian escorts, Australian Standard/Aegis possibilities, and new-build Invincibles for India or Brazil.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses long-term carrier security, Ocean, Queen Elizabeth replacements, Harrier/F-35B transition, and more Type 42/Type 23 escorts.
The transcript answers questions on Canadian escort evolution, Australian/Aegis options, and possible Indian or Brazilian Invincible purchases.
